summary: Gruber dissects NBC Universal's response to Apple's announcement that NBC content would be leaving iTunes, focusing on NBC's claim that 'the typical iPod contains a significant amount of illegally downloaded material.' He highlights the weaselly passive voice of 'it is estimated' as a rhetorical trick borrowed from the music industry's playbook of treating customers as criminals. He speculates that NBC's anti-piracy demands likely involved either restricting iPods to DRM-only content or adding spyware to iTunes. He also demolishes NBC's pricing demands by comparing their proposed $5-per-episode iTunes pricing to the far cheaper per-episode cost of DVD box sets.
key_points: NBC's claim about iPods containing 'illegally downloaded material' uses unsourced passive voice to present speculation as fact
NBC's anti-piracy demands likely amounted to either DRM-locking iPods or adding spyware to iTunes, both non-starters for Apple
NBC's desired $5-per-episode pricing would make a full season cost $100 via iTunes versus $40-60 for a DVD box set
The whole episode follows the content industry playbook of assuming customers are criminals and treating them with contempt
money_quotes: Love that passive voice: "it is estimated". Estimated by whom? Based on what evidence?
This is straight out of the music industry's playbook: assume your customers are criminals and treat them with contempt.
Needless to say, it's no wonder Apple told them to go fuck themselves.
At $5 per episode, it would cost about $100 to watch an entire season of a typical show via iTunes.
There's a lot of jackassery to pick apart in this short statement from NBC Universal.
key_insight: NBC's negotiating position combined unsourced piracy fearmongering with pricing demands that were objectively worse than existing DVD box set economics, making Apple's decision to cut them loose an obvious one.
